speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18th of December, 1996. The name of the survivor is Minia Jay. Name at birth-- Munter. My name is Bea Leskowicz. And we're conducting the interview in London, UK. And the language of the interview is English. Today is the 18th of December, 1996. We're interviewing Mrs. Minia Jay. My name is Bea Leskowicz and we're conducting the interview in London, UK. And the language of the interview is English. Can you tell me your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Minia Munter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-N-I-A M-U-N-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my name at birth, Minia Munter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is your name today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my name is Minia Jay. J-A-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was 13 years old when the war started. And I was the youngest in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born? I need the birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born please?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warta, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Warta please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. W-A-R-T-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we begin the interview by asking you-- can tell me about your first child memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was the youngest at home. We would have a large family of five sisters. We were five sisters and two brothers and my parents. And I also, as a child, I felt that there was a lot of anti-Semitism. And in school, when I went, it was the non-Jewish children and so on. But at home I felt sheltered. We were a religious family and we were a happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your parents and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Jacob Michael Munter. My mother was Frida Francis Munter, born Goodman. My brothers and sisters-- my eldest brother was Zalma Laib. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Afterwards came my sister, Hinda. Then I had a sister, Leah. And then I had another brother, Wolf Welwel. And then was my sister, Fremet, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my sister, Hadassa, and me, Minia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember from the time before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, we were a religious family. So, being a big family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents were happy. Busy people, but happy. And the whole family-- it all revolved around the family. I mean, I had an uncle and an aunt and they all also lived in the same area. And we always got together-- Shabbat-- and cousins. It was a big family. It was a wonderful life. As a child, I felt really good-- I mean, at home. I felt-- a sheltered family. I went to the Polish school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was a primary school. And from there, in the afternoon, I went to a Jewish school which was called [NON-ENGLISH] for learning religious-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] you know, to pray in Hebrew and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was like in a private house, just for certain children which was held there. It wasn't a very big school. But it was a very, very good school. The little bit which I know is from Yiddish [? guide ?] is all which I learned in those years at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home or in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any details? Do you remember what you learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We always got together. And we learned also all the nice songs-- the Israeli songs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, and we learned the Hatikvah and all these different-- we learned to dance the Horah, all the children. And every time we had a play, whenever it was before a holiday, they made a play up, where we all tried to act in it. And it was for Purim. You had the Purim story. And all the different festivals which we had. It was really very, very nice. As a child, I had it really good. I've got very good memories as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Zionist school? What was the orientation of that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The orientation of the school was purely a religious school-- mainly a religious school. Of course it was also Zionist, because we all were hoping one day to be in Israel and to get to know the country, and maybe one day to live there. But as small children, as we were in my age, we mainly were taught to read and write Yiddish and daven in Hebrew, pray.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the religious festivals at home? What did you celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we celebrated the festivals. They were wonderful, the festivals at home. It was such a community. It was a small town, but many came to Friday night. At a certain time, you didn't see anybody shopping or cooking or doing-- everything was done. The candles were alight. The men were running to the shtiebels or to the synagogues. It was really a wonderful feeling. I know that they had big problems with the government, in Poland with anti-Semitism and everything else. But when it came to holiday, everybody was just in their homes and getting together, the families, and going to the synagogue. My father always went to the shtiebel. For him, it was the shtiebel that he belonged. He was a Gerrer Hasid, so for him it was the most important, the shtiebel. My mother went to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what it meant at that time to be a Gerrer Hasid?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a really heart and soul a Gerrer Hasid. The meaning of it was, first of all, he was a religious man. And secondly, whenever he had a real problem, if something occurred in the family, he would go to see the Gerrer rabbi. This was very important for him. And he would go a few times a year to see the Gerrer rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things would he discuss with him? Do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything which was very problematic. Anything which occurred which was-- if somebody got very ill, or some big problems with the government, whatever, they went to the Gerrer rabbi. And everybody-- there wasn't such a thing as people-- religious people wouldn't go to visit the Gerrer rabbi. The Gerrer rabbi always had lots and lots of people around him. To get to the Gerrer rabbi was hard. To really arrange it beforehand, it wasn't always easy because the Jewish people were very, very united. They were very religious in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go with him to Gerrer rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. No. I would never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of your brothers? Did they accompany your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, but nobody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a bit about Warta? What was Warta like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warta was not a very big town. It was near a river, which ran actually into a German river. It was called Warta, the river. And my family lived there since generations. My grandfather, my great-grandfather-- I don't know how far it's going back. But they lived there, generations in this small town. And they were land-owners. They had land. And they lived from the land. We had grazing land. There was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] woodland and land altogether. And my grandparents built the house, which was a very big house for the children. One part belonged to us. One part belonged to my uncle, which was the younger brother of my father. And the middle part to my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a long house. It was a built differently, not like here, because you had these-- what are they called? You know, where the horses were driving, these big gates to go in. And all around were big sheds to keep all the corn and everything from the land. It was different. Were you involved as a kid in working as well? Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, not in-- I went to school. I went to school, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother help your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had somebody who was doing the main work. We had a couple, and also a child. They lived on the back of the house. The man looked after the horses. The woman looked after the cows and so on. And we had always help to do the work on the land. And whenever it came to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] harvest and so on, we always had a lot of people to do the work. My father just supervised it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house in a bit more in detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The house was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it had six rooms. And there was a big kitchen. You know, in these small towns and we had enormous kitchens. It was a nice house. Very bright light.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names? You said your uncle and your aunt lived there as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's name was Leiser Munter. And they had two sons and a daughter. And then was my aunt. And she also had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was a widow. Her husband died years ago. I don't remember her husband. But she lived a nice age until the war came, because the Germans killed her. And then she had children, many children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about your relationship to the other Polish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had friends when I was in school. But I don't know what it was. They never were very, very-- terribly friendly they were not with the Jewish children. I don't know why. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, right next to our house, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the entrance, near the entrance to the-- what do you call it? In Polish I know it. I don't know. You know, where you dove in-- the driveway. Where you drove in with the horses. Just in front of it, near it, they had put a square. It was like a little garden around this fence. And they had Jesus on the cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every May, the whole month of May, they would come around, just near our house, and they were singing all the songs. And whenever they were singing around, I would always cross the road and go on the other side. Because somehow you felt that as a Jewish girl, you were not accepted. They really didn't want you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was only, at the time, a school girl. So I realized afterwards that it was very, very difficult. It was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day we had a-- our roofs had to be redone, you know? And we had two men who were doing the work. And one of them was a bit of a joker, I think. I don't know why, or he did it specially. He painted Jesus black on the cross. And suddenly, they came around knocking at the door. Where is Mr. Munter? What's happened here? We had such a shock because we never knew that they're going to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we promised that this is going to be taken off and cleaned. And we made them completely new. We had it painted always fresh golden. It was nice and shiny. And everything was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But believe me, we were very worried about it because what did we need it for? They thought that we had it done, you know, like a [YIDDISH] you say in Yiddish, you know, as if we wanted to do something. Did you ever experience anti-Semitism in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what time of the year that to is-- I can't remember what time of the year, where they would come in and they would use pork, you know? The fat of the pork, and put it on the blackboard because it was a holiday of theirs. I don't remember which of it-- it was before Christmas. I don't know. And they would come and put some on us because they knew that we are not allowed to have pork, that we don't eat it. Things like that. This was the children's school, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other Jewish children were in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few. I can't remember how many, because we were quite a few Jewish people in the town. But now there's nobody there. Nobody's left in Warta. I never went back even to the town, because I have nobody there, no family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe one day I would have liked to go back. But it really doesn't-- until now, I haven't had the will to go because it's the memories and everything. I mean, what is the point of going to see the house you lived in when you know you have got no family there? What other things do you remember from that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I was a carefree girl, happy at home with all my family and all my friends who were near me. Were mainly with the Jewish children, you know, because we were in that area where we were, you know. I had some Polish friends as well, a few. Yes. At home, as I say, I was a happy girl at home. But, of course, when the war broke out it changed completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, for example, how you celebrated Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always Shabbat was such a lovely-- Friday night was the best night for my mother. I remember she always used to say-- Friday night, when we all sat down to the meal and everything was done-- because, being religious people, when the days were very short, my mother would already start Thursday, preparing for Shabbat. Because we would make, you know, [YIDDISH] in Yiddish. We wouldn't-- so everything was-- the table was beautifully laid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father-- that I must tell you. My father was a very, very good-natured man. And when he went to the shtiebel, he would never come home alone. He always brought one or two, what we called, you know, the poor man, like you call, you know, who had nowhere to go. And to be without a Shabbas? That didn't exist.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was always waiting until he saw-- there were always-- there were many poor people. And, of course, he always waited until he brought one or two people home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had to respect them so much. They would sit at the top of the table near my father. And sometimes they came-- they were dirty, you know. I mean, poor, what we used to call [YIDDISH], you know? And they had a cold. We were not supposed to even look at them, they were so respected. And they were sitting at the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was our life at home-- very, very charitable. When it comes when I will tell you about during the war, what happened, I will tell you how my father used to-- he wouldn't eat unless somebody else also had. There was a family living next to us with small children. And we still had-- because we had land, so we had still carrots and potatoes and cabbages and things like that. We still had food. And we wouldn't eat. My father wouldn't eat unless they would also have to eat, you know. Because some people didn't have anything during the war. The money was-- you couldn't buy anything for the money. And it was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we spoke mainly Yiddish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: As well, Yiddish. At home, we used to speak Yiddish. Yes. But we spoke Polish. You know, of course, we went to the Polish school and we studied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your parents met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that is a wonderful story actually, because my mother came from a larger town, from Pabianice, which is near Lodz. She came from a big family. And her sister-- she had only one sister and there were five brothers. Her sister-- it was all match made, you know. And also being such a religious family in Pabianice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she married a man, a very Hasidic man, who was studying, you know, like learning. He was supposed to just-- but turned out, not only was he a very studious man, that studied Talmud Torah, and so on, but it turned out, he was a wonderful man but very, very ill. He had heart problems. So my sister had a very difficult time. The parents kept them and so on. But just the same, when the family came, so on. So my dear parents decided, for their youngest daughter, which is my mother, she has to go in and she will be-- she also was match made. And that's when she was match made to my father. The Munter who had learned and lived in Warta. But my father was 28 and my mother was 18. Well, she was 17 and he was 27. But when they got married, she was 18, he was 28.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so they were match made. My mother fell in love with him, straight away. And he liked her so much. Anyway, all their life was a very happy life. They were really very happy together. And of course, they had this big family. So this turned out-- this is how my mother met my father. When they saw each other, they liked each other. My mother said-- she was always telling us how happy they were, even though it was a match made. It was like match made in heaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers and sisters? Were they already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother got married before the war. And he married, actually to a girl also in Pabianice, to a nice family. They had no children. But my sister married. And she lived in Lodz. And she had three children. She had a girl and two boys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Little, of course. They were little. Then I had my other sister, Lea. She got married and she had two little boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they find their husbands?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always match made. In our family, you couldn't marry any different. My father would never allow any of the girls-- of his daughters-- to speak to someone like they-- they couldn't meet unless they were introduced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- did people come and visit? Do you remember how these were arranged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when my-- I don't remember very much about my brother or my sister, because I was the very youngest at home. There was a great gap between me and my older sister or brother. But I remember one day, the window was open in the summertime, and my sister, Lea, people knew us. Someone came around and he started talking to her while the window was open. My father came in. She closed the window straightaway. This is how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And can you tell me a bit more-- you said about the holidays. You explained Shabbat. What about the other holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all the holidays were lovely. I mean, they were always-- every holiday had its own meaning. And the children were always dressed for Shabbas and for Yom Tov. And always for Pesach, new clothes. And for Rosh Hashanah again, different clothes, you know. Yes. And always going to the synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was wonderful, especially when I was a child. I still didn't feel this great-- if they had problems, I didn't know so much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the synagogue to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a lovely synagogue. I tell you, our small town, the synagogue was-- how should I compare it? Believe me, some of the areas here in London haven't got such a nice synagogue as that one. I'm not talking about [INAUDIBLE] synagogue or Kinloss, because they are large. But ours was also quite a nice large synagogue, with lovely windows, all stained. A beautiful synagogue it was-- really lovely. We only had one synagogue. The rest were all shtiebels.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many shtiebels were there in Warta?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know that, because there was-- where my father went was Gerrer shtiebel. And then there was Mizrahi, where my uncle, belonged to Mizrahi. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Mizrahi shtiebel? What was the difference between the two?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a difference in the degree of how religious. The Gerrer Hasid is one which only follows the Gerrer rabbi. And the Gerrer rabbi was a real Hasid. Mizrahi was a bit more modern. I mean, the Mizrahi-- my uncle already had a short beard cut. And it was more modern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father want you to follow the Gerrer Hasid as well? Did he talk about it at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew my father was a Gerrer Hasid. And Shabbat, for instance, after he went to synagogue and then came home-- especially after lunch-- there is always the three meals, you know, [YIDDISH]. But in between my father would always have this enormous, wonderful, you know, the [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the mishnah. They had enormous books. And he would start always reading a portion from it, Shabbat. That was mainly what he would do on a Shabbat. The family was coming together, the children, the cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change tapes.
